title: Discipleship, Part 3: Growing Together as Disciples of Jesus (Part 2)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 3 - Growing Together as Disciples of Jesus - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: 1. Quick Review: What Discipleship Is and Why It Matters
subpoints: Every Christian is a disciple making disciples simultaneously -- like water becoming ice and ice becoming water at 32 degrees
Cross-generational discipleship is essential: segmenting the church by age and affinity creates a problem for discipleship
Discipleship is the constant means God uses to preserve us until Christ returns
The goal: unity of faith, maturity, and the fullness of Christ (Ephesians 4:13)

outline.title: 2. How to Disciple One Another: Three Methods from Ephesians 4
subpoints: Speaking the truth in love (Ephesians 4:15) -- not 'calling it as it is' but truth wielded for growth, not harm
Every member of God's community doing his or her part (Ephesians 4:16) -- no bench players
Being filled with the Spirit (Ephesians 5:18-21) -- described concretely: speaking God's word, giving thanks, submitting to one another

outline.title: 3. Natural Discipleship Relationships
subpoints: Older and younger saints (Titus 2:1-8) -- the older man training the younger man; the older woman training the younger woman
Husband and wife (Ephesians 5; Genesis 2) -- the husband sanctifying and nourishing; the wife as his helper
Parents and children (Ephesians 6)
Pastor and congregation (Ephesians 4:11); pastors training future pastors (2 Timothy 2:1-2)

outline.title: 4. The Lord's Day as the Center of Discipleship
subpoints: The Lord's Day is the focal point from which discipleship flows throughout the week
Hebrews 10:24-25 -- not forsaking the assembly is a discipleship command, not just an attendance rule
The shift in the mid-20th century: worship became geared to unbelievers rather than building the saints
Come, stay, and invest -- rushing out each Sunday communicates you don't want people in your life

outline.title: 5. Practical Suggestions for Doing Life Together
subpoints: Schedule lunch together with someone from the church -- does not require a restaurant or expense
Read the Bible together using a simple approach like David Helm's One-to-One Bible Reading
Pray together and learn how to pray from each other
Work out together and talk about what you've been reading in Scripture
Have people over for meals -- hospitality does not require great fanfare
The only limitations are the Ten Commandments and your imagination

summary:
This third lesson in the Discipleship series continues the overview begun in Part 2, reviewing what discipleship is, why it matters, and now focusing on how to do it in practical, everyday life. Pastor Tito Lyro opens with a quick review, using the illustration of water and ice at 32 degrees to capture the mutuality of discipleship: every Christian is simultaneously being discipled and discipling others.

The lesson works through three methods of discipleship from Ephesians 4: speaking the truth in love (truth used to build up, never to harm), every member doing his or her part (no bench players in the church of Christ), and being filled with the Spirit (which Ephesians 5:18-21 describes not as a mystical experience but as concrete actions: speaking the word to one another, giving thanks, submitting to one another).

A section on natural discipleship relationships walks through Titus 2 (older men training younger men; older women training younger women), marriage (Ephesians 5), parenting (Ephesians 6), and the pastor-to-future-pastor relationship of 2 Timothy 2:1-2. The 44 unique 'one another' commands in the New Testament cover every relationship in the church.

The Lord's Day is identified as the center and focal point of the discipleship process -- not merely an attendance obligation but the peak from which the rest of the week flows. A substantial list of practical suggestions for doing life together concludes the lesson: scheduling lunch with someone, reading the Bible together, praying together, working out and talking about Scripture, hosting people for meals. The only limits, Pastor Lyro says, are the Ten Commandments and your imagination.
transcript:
## Discipleship, Part 3: Growing Together as Disciples of Jesus (Part 2)

*Pastor Tito Lyro -- Sunday School*

---

We're returning to Ephesians 4, considering verses 11 through 16, and we started last week in our series on discipleship.

The Lord has commissioned his church to make disciples. That's what the church is about -- that's the identity and the mission of the church. Everything else is secondary to the major mission: to make disciples. The mission of the church is not to provide food for the poor, not to provide clean water, not to seize social inequalities, not to bring racial reconciliation -- not that these are bad things, but as disciples are made, these things are addressed. They are not the primary mission. The primary mission is to make disciples, by baptizing through evangelism, and by teaching them to observe whatever the Lord has commanded.

Each one of us is called to be part of making disciples. Each one of us is a part of that mission.

### Quick Review

Last week we looked at five definitions of discipleship, all of which share two constants: discipleship is always aimed at Christ-likeness, and it is always centered on the Bible.

We also saw that every Christian is a disciple. When you ask who is a disciple, the answer is: every person who believes in Jesus Christ. Acts 11:26 uses "disciples" and "Christians" interchangeably. You are a disciple -- not something you become after a period of growth, but what you are from the moment of conversion.

I like the illustration of 32 degrees Fahrenheit. What happens at 32 degrees? Not only is water freezing, but ice is melting simultaneously -- both things happening at once. That's the disciple: you're being discipled and you're discipling, simultaneously, your whole life.

We also saw that discipleship involves: learning and loving God's word; pursuing peace and edifying one another; bearing with one another; encouraging holy living; and lighting a fire for love and good works in our hearts. And the goal (Ephesians 4:13): unity of faith, maturity, and the fullness of Christ.

And we saw the importance of cross-generational discipleship. Segmenting churches by age and affinity creates a big problem, since Paul tells Titus that the young and old must be interacting so that all can grow in Christ.

### How Should We Disciple One Another? Three Methods from Ephesians 4

**Method 1: Speaking the truth in love** (Ephesians 4:15).

> *But speaking the truth in love, may grow up in all things into him who is the head -- Christ.*

Who is speaking the truth in love? The saints who are being equipped for the work of ministry. The truth is spoken with the *purpose* of blessing those around you with growth in Jesus. This is not the same as "I call it as I see it." Speaking the truth in love is done so that the person to whom you're speaking grows in Jesus Christ. Truth can be used to hurt -- which is not the same as saying that the truth hurts. Sometimes it does hurt, like working out. But discipleship is truth used to build, not to wound.

**Method 2: Every member of God's community doing his or her part** (Ephesians 4:16).

> *From whom the whole body, joined and knit together by what every joint supplies, according to the effective working by which every part does its share, causes growth of the body for the edifying of itself in love.*

When people in the church are not growing in Christ, it's easy to blame the leadership. There is some blame there. But it is also every member's responsibility to disciple one another. There is no bench player in the Church of Jesus Christ. Everybody is in the game.

**Method 3: Being filled with the Spirit** (Ephesians 5:18-21).

Ultimately, we tend to think of being filled with the Spirit as a mystical subjective experience. But Paul describes it in concrete actions:

> *...speaking to one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord, giving thanks always for all things to God the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, submitting to one another in the fear of God.*

Being filled with the Spirit means: (1) speaking God's word to one another; (2) giving thanks; (3) submitting to one another. Not waiting actions -- going-forward actions.

Colossians 3:16 connects: *"Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly in all wisdom, teaching and admonishing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs."* The word of Christ actually dwells in us *when* we are sharing it with others. If you are not sharing the word with your brothers and sisters, you are a black hole of the word -- it comes in but never goes out.

### Natural Discipleship Relationships

Paul identifies several naturally occurring discipleship relationships:

**Older and younger saints** (Titus 2:1-8) -- the aging out and the aging in of the church. Older men train younger men; older women train younger women. This is not fixed by calendar age but by relative maturity in faith. Our youth will reflect our older men and women.

**Husband and wife** (Ephesians 5; Genesis 2) -- the husband sanctifies and nourishes; the wife is his helper. Discipleship goes both ways.

**Parents and children** (Ephesians 6).

**Pastor and congregation** (Ephesians 4:11).

**Pastors training future pastors** (2 Timothy 2:1-2) -- Paul to Timothy to faithful men to others.

And beyond these, every relationship in the church can be a discipleship relationship. There are 44 unique "one another" commands in the New Testament, independent of affinity or age.

### The Lord's Day as the Center of Discipleship

The Lord's Day with public and private worship and relationships is the central pillar of discipleship. Everything flows from it.

Hebrews 10:23-25:
> *Let us hold fast the confession of our hope without wavering, for he who promised is faithful. And let us consider one another in order to stir up love and good works, not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as is the manner of some, but exhorting one another, and so much the more as you see the Day approaching.*

The concept of the weekend has taken over. Sunday is thought of as our day to relax. But Sunday was given for the worship of the Lord and for discipling one another. The Lord's Day is the peak; the valley of the week flows from one Lord's Day into the next.

If every Sunday you have a reason to rush out of here, then the Lord's Day is not properly prioritized. God has given us the entire day to delight in him and to help us grow in him. Come, stay, talk with each other. If you sit down, worship, and take off, you communicate that you don't want anyone in your life.

The mid-20th century church shifted from understanding Lord's Day worship as God's people gathering before him, to understanding it as an evangelistic opportunity for unbelievers. Every sermon became a "John 3:16 sermon." The worship service was oriented to the outsider rather than the believer. That is not the biblical pattern.

### Practical Suggestions for Doing Life Together

Here are some suggestions for doing life together in the context of fellowship -- these can be taken or left. The only limitations to what you can do in discipleship are the Ten Commandments and your imagination:

- **Schedule lunch together** with someone from the church during the week. Doesn't have to be a restaurant -- you can use the church microwave. No expense required.
- **Read the Bible together.** You don't need much preparation. Just sit down with someone and read. David Helm wrote a small book called *One-to-One Bible Reading* with simple strategies for how to do this.
- **Pray together.** Just get together to pray and listen to each other pray. Hearing how someone else approaches the Lord teaches you to pray.
- **Work out together.** If you're going to the gym anyway, invite someone else along and talk about what you've been reading in your devotions.
- **Meet at the park** when the weather permits, let the kids play, and talk about the last sermon or something you've been reading.
- **Have people over for meals.** No fanfare required. Just open your house.

All of us in the church -- we are only developing as a body as our most slowly-developing part is developing. Like a limb that is atrophied: something has to be done. The goal: *"that we all may grow up in all things into him who is the head -- Christ."* (Ephesians 4:15)

No disciples left behind. Let's pray.
